juntendo-12-month.title: Juntendo University 12-Month Interim Results
juntendo-12-month.subtitle: Sustained kidney and liver volume reduction with ketogenic metabolic therapy
juntendo-12-month.design: Single-arm, open-label clinical trial (interim analysis). First clinical investigation in Asia of ketogenic metabolic intervention for ADPKD. 10 patients enrolled, 7 completed 12 months. Mayo Imaging Class C or higher. Median age 44 years, median baseline eGFR 57 mL/min/1.73 m2. MRI-based volumetric measurements at baseline, 3 months, and 12 months.
juntendo-12-month.intervention: Structured ketogenic diet with multidisciplinary supervision, individualized dietitian guidance, KetoCitra supplementation (BHB + alkaline citrate), digital self-monitoring of ketones, glucose, and nutrients.

results.label: TKV
results.detail: Paired t-test p=0.026; Wilcoxon p=0.016. In a population where 5-10% annual growth is expected, kidneys actually shrank.

results.label: Liver Volume
results.detail: At both 3 and 12 months (Wilcoxon p=0.049 at 12 months).

results.label: eGFR
results.detail: No significant change vs. historical pre-trial decline of approximately -5.6 mL/min/1.73 m2/year.

results.label: Dose-Response
results.detail: Mean blood ketone levels at 3 months showed strong negative correlation with TKV change (Spearman rho = -0.86, p=0.012). Higher ketones = more kidney shrinkage.

juntendo-12-month.comparison: Tolvaptan (TEMPO 3:4) reduced annual TKV growth from +5.51%/year (placebo) to +2.80%/year — kidneys still grow. This ketogenic intervention achieved actual TKV decrease.
juntendo-12-month.conclusion: First long-term clinical trial data showing ketogenic metabolic therapy can reverse kidney growth in ADPKD. The strong dose-response relationship between ketone levels and kidney volume reduction supports a causal mechanism. Enrollment completed with >50 participants plus a control group; final results expected in approximately one year.

juntendo-3-month.title: Juntendo University 3-Month Interim Results
juntendo-3-month.subtitle: 5% kidney volume reduction in 90 days with KetoCitra and nutritional changes
juntendo-3-month.design: Controlled intervention study with intervention and control groups. One-year study; these are 3-month interim results. Moderate-stage ADPKD patients.
juntendo-3-month.intervention: KetoCitra supplementation + Ren-Nu program (education on ketogenic nutrition and lifestyle changes) vs. control group receiving standard care.

results.label: TKV Change
results.detail: Intervention group had a 5.01% kidney volume reduction compared to an 8.03% increase in the control group — statistically significant.

results.label: eGFR Change
results.detail: Intervention: +0.59 mL/min/1.73 m2 (trend toward stabilization). Control: -1.57 mL/min/1.73 m2 (continued decline).

results.label: Adherence
results.detail: Zero participants discontinued the intervention.

juntendo-3-month.conclusion: A 5% kidney volume reduction in just 90 days is unprecedented in ADPKD research. This controlled study confirms results are not due to natural variation, as the control group showed expected kidney growth. 100% adherence suggests the program is practical and sustainable for patients.

cleveland-clinic.title: Cleveland Clinic KetoCitra Trial
cleveland-clinic.subtitle: Major academic center validates ketogenic nutrition approach for ADPKD
cleveland-clinic.design: Prospective, interventional clinical trial. Up to 20 ADPKD enrollees. 3-month dietary intervention with assessments at baseline and post-intervention. NCT07454174.
cleveland-clinic.intervention: Ren-Nu structured ketogenic nutrition program + KetoCitra (medical food). Includes continuous glucose monitoring, individualized dietitian support, and digital monitoring tools.
cleveland-clinic.conclusion: This trial represents an important milestone: the first study of KetoCitra and the Ren-Nu program conducted at a major academic medical center independent of the product manufacturer. Led by Dr. Richard Fatica, Vice Chair of Nephrology at Cleveland Clinic, the trial will provide rigorous, independent evaluation of the approach in a controlled clinical setting. Results expected in 2027.

ren-nu-real-world.title: Ren-Nu Real-World Outcomes (103 Patients)
ren-nu-real-world.subtitle: eGFR improvement, reduced pain, and fewer medications in real-world ADPKD program
ren-nu-real-world.design: Retrospective, longitudinal, baseline-controlled program evaluation. 103 ADPKD participants with analyzable data from 151 completers. 3-month intervention plus 1-month orientation. Data collected March 2021 - September 2023. Eligibility: confirmed ADPKD + eGFR > 30 mL/min/1.73 m2. Demographics: age 50 +/- 10.7 years, 68.9% female, BMI 25.0 +/- 4.4, baseline eGFR 58.4 +/- 21.07.
ren-nu-real-world.intervention: Ren-Nu Program: KetoCitra daily (5.3g BHB, 3.5g citric acid, 600mg potassium, 300mg calcium, 250mg magnesium, 51 mEq alkaline base, sodium-free) + very-low-carb, high-fat, moderate-protein ketogenic diet (target blood BHB >= 0.5 mmol/L) + plant-focused, kidney-safe nutrition (low oxalate, no inorganic phosphate additives) + fully remote delivery with weekly group meetings + 3 individualized nutrition coaching sessions.

results.label: eGFR
results.detail: Increased from 58.4 to 61.6 mL/min/1.73 m2 (n=77).

results.label: BMI
results.detail: Significant reduction (n=35).

results.label: Blood Pressure Meds
results.detail: Of 24 patients on antihypertensives: 5 discontinued, 7 decreased dosage, 11 no change, 1 increased (P=0.039).

results.label: Kidney Pain
results.detail: Of 27 patients with pain: 52% experienced reduction, 0% increase (P<0.0001).

results.label: Lipids
results.detail: Total cholesterol, HDL, LDL, triglycerides all remained stable. Potassium and bicarbonate unchanged.

ren-nu-real-world.conclusion: The largest real-world dataset on ketogenic metabolic intervention in ADPKD demonstrates clinically meaningful improvements in kidney function, pain, and medication burden. The safety profile is reassuring — no adverse lipid changes despite the high-fat diet, and potassium/bicarbonate levels remained normal despite supplementation. These results support the rationale for ongoing randomized controlled trials.

keto-adpkd-post-hoc.title: Post-Hoc Analysis: KETO-ADPKD Significant TKV Decrease
keto-adpkd-post-hoc.subtitle: Re-analysis confirms ketogenic diet significantly reduced kidney volume in compliant patients
keto-adpkd-post-hoc.design: Post-hoc analysis of the KETO-ADPKD randomized controlled trial data (66 patients, 3 months). Focused on patients who achieved sustained ketosis and analyzed TKV changes with refined methodology.
keto-adpkd-post-hoc.intervention: Same as KETO-ADPKD: ketogenic diet (<30g carbs/day) for 3 months vs. standard diet control.

results.label: TKV
results.detail: When analyzed with refined methodology and focus on ketosis-adherent participants, the kidney volume reduction reached statistical significance — upgrading the original trial's 'trend' finding.

keto-adpkd-post-hoc.comparison: The original KETO-ADPKD trial reported a non-significant trend (-0.55% KD vs +0.79% control). This post-hoc analysis with improved statistical methodology demonstrates the reduction was indeed significant in adherent participants.
keto-adpkd-post-hoc.conclusion: This re-analysis resolves the key limitation of the original KETO-ADPKD trial. The original 3-month period was considered too short to detect TKV changes, but refined analysis shows the effect was present and statistically significant. Combined with the Juntendo 12-month data showing even larger effects, this strengthens the evidence that ketogenic interventions directly reduce kidney volume.

ketosis-cell-metabolism-2019.title: Ketosis Ameliorates Renal Cyst Growth in PKD
ketosis-cell-metabolism-2019.subtitle: First evidence that ketogenic interventions slow cyst growth in animal models
ketosis-cell-metabolism-2019.design: Preclinical study using multiple PKD animal models (orthologous and non-orthologous). Tested time-restricted feeding, ketogenic diet, and acute BHB administration.
ketosis-cell-metabolism-2019.intervention: Three interventions tested: (1) Time-restricted feeding (limiting food availability), (2) Ketogenic diet (high-fat, very-low-carb), (3) Oral BHB (beta-hydroxybutyrate) supplementation.

results.label: Ketogenic Diet
results.detail: Ketogenic diet significantly slowed cyst growth and preserved kidney function in PKD rat models.

results.label: Time-Restricted Feeding
results.detail: Intermittent fasting slowed PKD progression even without caloric restriction.

results.label: BHB Supplementation
results.detail: Acute BHB administration alone was sufficient to slow cyst growth, demonstrating the ketone body itself is the therapeutic agent.

results.label: Mechanism
results.detail: Ketosis inhibits mTOR signaling and activates AMPK in cyst-lining cells, reducing proliferation and fluid secretion.

ketosis-cell-metabolism-2019.conclusion: This landmark paper established the scientific foundation for all subsequent ketogenic diet research in PKD. By showing that BHB alone (not just dietary changes) could slow cyst growth, it opened the door to supplement-based approaches like KetoCitra. Published in the high-impact journal Cell Metabolism, it sparked worldwide interest in metabolic therapies for PKD.

food-restriction-2016.title: Food Restriction Ameliorates PKD Development
food-restriction-2016.subtitle: First demonstration that dietary intervention can slow polycystic kidney disease
food-restriction-2016.publication: American Journal of Physiology - Renal Physiology
food-restriction-2016.design: Preclinical study in an orthologous ADPKD mouse model (mosaic conditional Pkd1 knockout). Compared ad libitum feeding vs. mild food restriction (23% reduction in caloric intake).
food-restriction-2016.intervention: Mild food restriction (23% caloric reduction) in an orthologous PKD mouse model.

results.label: Cyst Growth
results.detail: Food-restricted mice had dramatically less cystic disease compared to ad libitum-fed controls.

results.label: Kidney Function
results.detail: Kidney function was significantly better preserved in the food-restricted group.

results.label: Proliferation
results.detail: Cyst-lining cell proliferation was significantly lower with food restriction.

food-restriction-2016.conclusion: This was the first published evidence that a simple dietary change could slow PKD progression. It challenged the prevailing assumption that PKD was purely a genetic disease unresponsive to lifestyle intervention. This paper directly inspired the subsequent ketogenic diet research — if reducing food intake helps, could mimicking the metabolic state of fasting (ketosis) be even more effective?

crystals-citrate-2019.title: Microcrystals Worsen PKD, Citrate is Protective
crystals-citrate-2019.subtitle: Crystal deposits trigger new cyst formation; citrate supplementation prevents it
crystals-citrate-2019.design: Preclinical study examining the role of microcrystal deposits in PKD cystogenesis and the protective effect of citrate supplementation in PKD animal models.
crystals-citrate-2019.intervention: Citrate supplementation in drinking water of PKD mice. Compared crystal formation and cyst development with and without citrate.

results.label: Crystal-Cyst Connection
results.detail: Microcrystal deposits in kidney tubules trigger new cyst formation. PKD kidneys are more susceptible to crystal-induced damage.

results.label: Citrate Protection
results.detail: Citrate supplementation prevented crystal formation and reduced cyst development in PKD models.

results.label: Mechanism
results.detail: Crystals cause tubular cell injury and dedifferentiation, creating conditions that promote cystogenesis in genetically susceptible kidneys.

crystals-citrate-2019.conclusion: This paper provides the scientific rationale for including citrate in KetoCitra. PKD patients are prone to kidney stones and microcrystal formation, which can trigger new cysts. By supplementing with citrate (a natural crystal inhibitor), this damaging cycle can be interrupted. Combined with the BHB component targeting cyst growth directly, the dual-action approach of KetoCitra addresses two distinct pathological mechanisms.

ketosis-diabetic-ckd.title: Sustained Ketosis Improves Kidney Function in Diabetic CKD
ketosis-diabetic-ckd.subtitle: Post-hoc analysis shows dose-response between ketosis levels and eGFR improvement in type 2 diabetes
ketosis-diabetic-ckd.institution: Virta Health / Indiana University / Ohio State University
ketosis-diabetic-ckd.design: Post-hoc latent class trajectory analysis from the Virta Health continuous care intervention (CCI) trial (NCT02519309). 248 CCI participants and 87 usual care controls with type 2 diabetes followed for 2+ years. Compared sustained nutritional ketosis (SNK) group vs. moderate/low/unknown ketosis groups vs. usual care. Subgroup analysis of 23 CCI participants with baseline CKD stage 3+.
ketosis-diabetic-ckd.intervention: Continuous care intervention with nutritional ketosis (individualized very-low-carb diet targeting blood BHB >= 0.5 mmol/L) with remote physician and health coach support.

results.label: eGFR (sustained ketosis)
results.detail: Sustained nutritional ketosis (SNK) subgroup (n=17) showed eGFR slope of +3.38 mL/min/1.73 m2/year vs. -0.69 in usual care (between-group difference 4.06, p=0.01).

results.label: eGFR (all CCI)
results.detail: Overall CCI group: +0.91 mL/min/1.73 m2/year (p=0.03). Usual care: -0.68 mL/min/1.73 m2/year. Between-group difference 1.59 (p=0.06).

results.label: Dose-Response
results.detail: SNK: +3.38, Moderate: +1.09, Low: +0.20, Unknown: +0.22, Usual care: -0.69 mL/min/yr. Higher sustained ketosis associated with greater eGFR improvement.

ketosis-diabetic-ckd.comparison: While this study is in diabetic CKD (not PKD specifically), it demonstrates that sustained nutritional ketosis preserves and improves kidney function. The dose-response relationship mirrors findings in ADPKD trials. Note: the SNK subgroup (n=17) is small; the overall CCI group result (+0.91) is more robust.
ketosis-diabetic-ckd.conclusion: This post-hoc analysis from a non-PKD population shows that sustained ketosis is associated with improved kidney function. The sustained ketosis subgroup gained +3.38 mL/min/year in eGFR (vs. -0.69 in usual care). Among 23 participants with CKD stage 3+, mean eGFR improved from 49.9 to 64.7 at 2 years. The dose-response pattern supports a causal relationship, though the small subgroup sizes warrant cautious interpretation.

ohio-state-volek.title: Ohio State Well-Formulated Ketogenic Diet Trial
ohio-state-volek.subtitle: 52-week keto diet with continuous ketone monitoring and MRI imaging in high-risk ADPKD
ohio-state-volek.institution: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
ohio-state-volek.design: Single-arm, open-label interventional trial (NCT06325644). 20 ADPKD patients at high risk for rapid progression (Mayo Class 1C-1E). Funded by the U.S. Department of Defense (CDMRP-PR212399-G). Assessments at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months including MRI for kidney volume and GFR.
ohio-state-volek.intervention: Well-formulated ketogenic diet for 52 weeks targeting blood ketones >0.5 mM (<50g/day carbohydrate, ~1.5 g/kg protein, remainder fat from whole foods). Monitoring includes daily finger-stick ketones (KetoMojo), continuous glucose/ketone monitor (CGM/CKM replaced every 2 weeks), blood draws, DEXA scanning, and MRI.
ohio-state-volek.comparison: First long-duration (1 year) ketogenic diet trial for ADPKD with continuous ketone monitoring. Unlike the Juntendo trial which uses KetoCitra supplementation, this study tests diet alone without exogenous ketone support.
ohio-state-volek.conclusion: Led by Jeff Volek — one of the world's leading ketogenic diet researchers — this DoD-funded trial represents the first U.S.-based, year-long ketogenic intervention in ADPKD. The use of continuous ketone monitoring and MRI-based htTKV measurement will provide the most detailed longitudinal data on ketosis levels and kidney volume change. Primary completion estimated December 2026.

systematic-review-2026.title: Systematic Review: Ketogenic Dietary Interventions for ADPKD
systematic-review-2026.subtitle: First comprehensive synthesis of all human keto-ADPKD studies confirms metabolic feasibility, calls for longer trials
systematic-review-2026.design: Systematic review using the SWiM (Synthesis Without Meta-Analysis) approach. Searched three databases for human studies of ketogenic dietary interventions in ADPKD. Risk of bias assessed using Cochrane RoB 2.0, Newcastle-Ottawa scale, and ROBINS-I tool.
systematic-review-2026.intervention: Reviewed 8 studies employing ketogenic diets, intermittent fasting, and time-restricted feeding in ADPKD patients.

results.label: Weight/Anthropometrics
results.detail: KDIs linked to weight loss and improved anthropometric profiles across 9 of the reviewed interventions.

results.label: Blood Pressure
results.detail: Blood pressure remained stable in 5 interventions. Lipids (cholesterol, HDL, LDL, triglycerides) also unchanged — confirming safety.

results.label: eGFR
results.detail: eGFR was higher in 4 interventions or stable after intervention. No studies showed eGFR decline.

results.label: htTKV
results.detail: Evidence for renal structural change was inconsistent, limited by short intervention duration and small sample sizes.

systematic-review-2026.comparison: This is the first formal systematic review of all ketogenic interventions in ADPKD, synthesizing evidence from KETO-ADPKD, RESET-PKD, Juntendo, Ren-Nu, TRE, and other studies.
systematic-review-2026.conclusion: The review confirms that ketogenic interventions are metabolically feasible and safe in ADPKD — no adverse effects on lipids, kidney function, or blood pressure. Current evidence supports metabolic feasibility more consistently than renal disease modification. The authors emphasize that longer trials with appropriate comparators are needed before KDIs can be formally recommended. Notably, the review was published before the Juntendo 12-month data (WCN 2026) which subsequently demonstrated significant TKV reduction.

nutritional-adequacy-2026.title: Nutritional Adequacy of Ketogenic Diets for ADPKD
nutritional-adequacy-2026.subtitle: Well-planned keto diets can meet ADPKD nutritional requirements with minimal supplementation
nutritional-adequacy-2026.design: Nutritional analysis and meal plan development study. Developed an ADPKD-specific ketogenic meal plan (PKD-Keto) plus modified versions targeting nephrolithiasis risk and hyperkalemia risk. Compared nutritional adequacy against a reference CKD diet and dietary intake targets. Included cost analysis.
nutritional-adequacy-2026.intervention: PKD-Keto meal plans designed following ADPKD, CKD, and healthy eating guidelines. Macronutrient targets: 10% carbohydrate, 75% fat, 15% protein of total caloric intake.

results.label: Macronutrients
results.detail: All caloric and macronutrient targets were achieved in the PKD-Keto meal plans.

results.label: Micronutrients
results.detail: All micronutrients adequate except iodine (all plans), iron (females 19-50), and zinc (males) — easily addressed with supplementation.

results.label: Cost
results.detail: PKD-Keto plans slightly higher cost than reference CKD diet, but no difference based on socioeconomic area.

results.label: Safety Modifications
results.detail: Modified versions successfully addressed kidney stone risk (low oxalate) and hyperkalemia risk while maintaining ketogenic ratios.

nutritional-adequacy-2026.comparison: Addresses a key clinical concern: whether ketogenic diets can meet the unique nutritional requirements of ADPKD patients who may have reduced kidney function, stone risk, and electrolyte sensitivities.
nutritional-adequacy-2026.conclusion: This study from the University of Sydney demonstrates that a well-planned ketogenic diet tailored for ADPKD is nutritionally adequate and can be safely adapted for patients with kidney stone or hyperkalemia risk. The authors recommend supplementation of iodine, iron, and zinc for long-term use. This practical guidance supports the clinical implementation of ketogenic interventions being studied in ongoing trials.
